longTailKeywords: lateral knee mass that disappears with flexion
_meta.authoringNotes: Pippetti classification and prevalence figures cross-checked against Glasgow 1993 (Br J Sports Med) and Reagan 1989 (Arthroscopy). Surgical outcomes reflect arthroscopic decompression + meniscal repair literature (KSSTA, AJSM). ICD-10 M23.0 (cystic meniscus).

h1Title: Lateral Meniscus Cyst (Parameniscal Cyst): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: A lateral meniscus cyst is a fluid-filled sac that forms next to a horizontal cleavage tear of the lateral meniscus in the knee, where synovial fluid is forced one-way through the tear and pools in the surrounding tissue. It accounts for roughly 5-7% of all lateral meniscus tears (versus only about 1% on the medial side) and is most often seen in runners, soccer players, and pivot-sport athletes in their 20s to 40s. The hallmark is a palpable, tense lump along the outer joint line that disappears or shrinks when the knee bends — known as Pisani's sign. Untreated, the underlying tear can drive accelerated lateral compartment osteoarthritis. Combined arthroscopic cyst decompression with meniscal repair or partial meniscectomy achieves symptom relief in 85-95% of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: 5-7% of lateral meniscus tears develop a parameniscal cyst (vs ~1% medial)
keyStats.demographics: Male-to-female ratio approximately 3:1; peak incidence age 20-40
keyStats.avgAge: Most patients present between ages 20 and 50, often after a twisting sports injury
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 4-5 per 100,000 person-years in active populations

definition: A lateral meniscus cyst (ICD-10: M23.0, cystic meniscus) is a localized collection of synovial-like fluid that forms in the soft tissue adjacent to a torn lateral meniscus, almost always in continuity with a horizontal cleavage tear. The accepted mechanism is the one-way valve theory: rotational and compressive load drives synovial fluid through the meniscal tear, but the irregular tear flap prevents return flow, so fluid accumulates outside the meniscal rim and forms a parameniscal cyst. The Pippetti classification grades severity from I (intrameniscal, no extra-articular extension) through II (extension to the capsule) to III (extension beyond the capsule into the subcutaneous tissue, producing the classic visible lump). Lateral cysts are five to seven times more common than medial ones because the lateral meniscus bears about 60% of compartment load and is structurally less attached to the capsule. The condition is managed by orthopaedic surgeons with sports-medicine training; rheumatology, musculoskeletal radiology, and physical therapy are commonly involved. Unaddressed, the underlying tear and altered load mechanics predispose to early lateral compartment osteoarthritis within 5-10 years.

typesClassification.description: Cystic change is confined within the substance of the meniscus itself. There is no extra-articular mass; symptoms are typically mechanical joint-line pain without a palpable lump.
The cyst tracks from the torn meniscus to the joint capsule. A deep, sometimes palpable swelling appears at the joint line, particularly with knee extension.
The cyst extends beyond the capsule into the subcutaneous tissue. A visible, palpable mass on the outer knee is present, classically disappearing in flexion (Pisani's sign).
Cysts larger than 3 cm or those that track proximally or distally along the iliotibial band. They can mimic soft-tissue tumours and may compress the common peroneal nerve in rare cases.

primarySymptoms: A firm, tender lump along the outer (lateral) joint line of the knee that the patient or examiner can feel, typically 1-3 cm in size.
The lump shrinks or disappears when the knee is bent to 45-90 degrees and reappears on full extension — this is Pisani's sign, present in 60-80% of grade II-III cysts.
Lateral joint-line pain that worsens with twisting, pivoting, squatting, descending stairs, or running on uneven ground.
Mechanical symptoms such as catching, clicking, or transient locking of the knee, reflecting the underlying horizontal cleavage tear.
A sensation of fullness or pressure on the outside of the knee, especially after prolonged standing, walking, or athletic activity.
Mild intermittent effusion (knee swelling) that fluctuates with activity level, rather than the large tense effusion of an acute ACL injury.
Pain that is provoked by McMurray's test with valgus stress and external rotation, reproducing lateral meniscus loading.
Reduced ability to squat fully, with discomfort localized to the lateral joint line rather than the front of the knee.
Symptoms that develop gradually over weeks to months in pivot-sport athletes, often without a clear single injury, or that follow a remembered twisting episode.
earlyWarningSigns: A new pea-sized firm spot on the outer knee that the patient first notices when crossing legs or palpating the joint
Lateral joint-line ache after running that takes longer than usual to settle (more than 24-48 hours)
Faint clicking on the outer side of the knee with deep squatting or stair descent in a previously asymptomatic athlete
A sensation that the knee gives way briefly during cutting maneuvers without true buckling
emergencySigns: Sudden numbness, foot-drop, or weakness in dorsiflexion of the foot — possible common peroneal nerve compression by a large lateral cyst requires urgent imaging and decompression
Rapidly enlarging firm mass over weeks with night pain or systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss) — soft-tissue sarcoma must be excluded with MRI and possible biopsy
Hot, red, exquisitely tender lateral knee with fever — septic bursitis or infected cyst needs same-day evaluation and aspiration with culture
Acute locked knee that cannot be passively extended — likely displaced bucket-handle tear requiring urgent arthroscopy

causes.cause: Horizontal cleavage tear of the lateral meniscus
causes.description: The dominant cause. Repetitive shear and compressive load create a horizontal split in the body or posterior horn. Synovial fluid is forced through the tear and accumulates in surrounding tissue, generating the cyst. Almost every parameniscal cyst has a corresponding meniscal tear on MRI.

The torn meniscal flap acts as a check valve: knee flexion-extension cycles drive synovial fluid outward into the cyst, but the flap blocks return. Fluid pressure within the cyst rises, producing the tense palpable mass and the position-dependent Pisani sign.

causes.cause: Repetitive pivoting and twisting load in sport
causes.description: Soccer, basketball, tennis, running with cutting, and martial arts repeatedly stress the lateral meniscus. The lateral meniscus is more mobile and carries about 60% of compartment load, making it more vulnerable to cleavage tears than the medial side.

A discoid meniscus is thicker and disc-shaped rather than crescent-shaped, predisposing to horizontal cleavage tears in childhood and adolescence. Prevalence of discoid lateral meniscus is 3-5% in Asian populations and around 1% in Western series.

A single rotational injury with the foot planted (e.g., a soccer cutting move) can produce an acute horizontal tear that subsequently develops into a parameniscal cyst over weeks to months as fluid pumps through the tear.

In patients over 40, age-related meniscal degeneration produces horizontal cleavage tears with minimal trauma. These tears can also pump fluid into a parameniscal cyst, although such cysts are often smaller and less symptomatic than post-traumatic ones.

riskFactors.description: Soccer, basketball, rugby, tennis, squash, and martial arts repeatedly load the lateral meniscus in rotation. Active athletes account for the majority of post-traumatic parameniscal cysts.
Men are affected approximately three times more often than women, partly reflecting higher participation in cutting sports and partly higher absolute joint loads.
Most symptomatic cysts present between 20 and 50 years. Younger patients tend to have post-traumatic cysts; older patients have degenerative cleavage tears with smaller cysts.

Congenitally thickened, disc-shaped lateral meniscus increases horizontal cleavage tear risk significantly. Up to 30% of pediatric and adolescent lateral meniscal pathology involves a discoid variant.

riskFactors.factor: Previous lateral meniscus injury or surgery
riskFactors.description: A prior partial meniscectomy or untreated lateral tear leaves residual meniscal disorganization, raising recurrence and parameniscal cyst risk on the same side.
Construction, plumbing, flooring, and farming work that demand repeated deep knee flexion under load impose shear forces on the lateral meniscus and increase cleavage-tear incidence.

Bow-legged alignment shifts load medially, but in athletes with valgus thrust or dynamic lateral overload, biomechanical alignment can disproportionately stress the lateral compartment and contribute to tear formation.

riskFactors.factor: Obesity (BMI greater than or equal to 30)
riskFactors.description: Higher BMI increases joint compressive load; in middle-aged adults it is associated with greater meniscal degeneration and horizontal cleavage tear prevalence on MRI.

affectedDemographics: Most common in 20-50 year olds, with a peak in active 25-40 year old males in pivot sports
Discoid lateral meniscus and associated lateral cysts are more common in East Asian populations (Japanese, Korean), where prevalence of discoid meniscus reaches 3-5%
Runners, soccer players, basketball players, and tennis players account for a disproportionate share of post-traumatic cysts
Children and adolescents with persistent lateral knee pain and a popping knee should be evaluated for discoid meniscus with secondary parameniscal cyst
Sedentary adults over 50 may develop smaller degenerative cysts that are often asymptomatic and incidentally found on MRI
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with a focused history (twisting mechanism, pivot-sport exposure, mechanical symptoms) and physical examination. The decisive examination findings are a tender lump on the lateral joint line that demonstrates Pisani's sign — disappearance with knee flexion to 45-90 degrees — and a positive McMurray's test with valgus stress and external rotation reproducing lateral pain. MRI is the diagnostic gold standard: a well-defined, T2-hyperintense, lobulated parameniscal collection contiguous with a horizontal cleavage tear of the lateral meniscus is diagnostic, and MRI sensitivity for the underlying tear exceeds 95% on 1.5T or 3T scanners. High-resolution musculoskeletal ultrasound is a useful and radiation-free alternative; it identifies the cyst, demonstrates compressibility with probe pressure, and guides aspiration. Plain radiographs are usually normal in early disease but should be obtained to rule out other lateral knee masses (osteochondroma, lateral tibial plateau lesions) and to assess alignment and existing compartment osteoarthritis. Critical differentials include lateral collateral ligament ganglion, popliteal artery aneurysm (always check pulses — a pulsatile lateral mass demands urgent vascular imaging), proximal tibiofibular joint ganglion, soft-tissue lipoma, and rarely soft-tissue sarcoma. When the imaging picture is atypical, when there is rapid growth, or when systemic symptoms accompany the lump, MRI with gadolinium and surgical biopsy take priority over routine arthroscopy.

diagnosticTests.test: Knee MRI (1.5T or 3T, non-contrast, fat-saturated T2 sequences)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard imaging. Demonstrates the parameniscal cyst as a well-defined T2-hyperintense collection contiguous with a horizontal cleavage tear of the lateral meniscus, and grades extension per Pippetti classification.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 25-40 minute scan in a magnet, lying still with the knee in a coil. No injections, no radiation. Results are typically read within 24-48 hours by a musculoskeletal radiologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the cyst, confirms fluid contents, demonstrates compressibility, and can guide aspiration with or without steroid injection. Useful when MRI is not immediately available or as an in-office adjunct.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician applies gel and a probe over the lateral knee while the patient lies supine and then with the knee in 45 degrees of flexion. The scan takes 10-15 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.test: Standing weight-bearing knee X-rays (AP, lateral, skyline, Rosenberg view)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses lateral compartment joint-space narrowing, alignment, and rules out other bony masses such as osteochondroma, lateral plateau lesions, or fracture.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Quick standing radiographs taken in 5-10 minutes. Some weight-bearing required; not painful. Results read same day in most centres.

diagnosticTests.test: Pisani's sign and McMurray's test on physical examination
diagnosticTests.purpose: Pisani's sign — the lateral mass disappears or shrinks with knee flexion — is roughly 60-80% sensitive and very specific for parameniscal cyst. McMurray's test localizes lateral meniscal pathology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner palpates the lateral knee with the leg straight, then bends the knee to 45-90 degrees while continuing to palpate. McMurray's involves passive knee flexion-extension with rotation. Mild discomfort during testing is normal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms cyst contents (clear viscous synovial fluid, not blood or pus), provides immediate symptom relief, and can be combined with corticosteroid injection. Cytology rules out atypical lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-15 minute in-office procedure under local anaesthetic. A fine needle is guided into the cyst under ultrasound and the fluid (typically 2-10 mL) is withdrawn. Mild soreness for 24 hours is normal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for ambiguous tears or post-surgical knees where standard MRI cannot reliably distinguish residual tear from scar. Improves sensitivity for subtle horizontal cleavage tears.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A radiologist injects dilute gadolinium into the knee joint under fluoroscopy; the patient then has a standard MRI within 30 minutes. Some pressure during injection is normal.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is tailored to symptom severity, cyst size, the underlying meniscal tear pattern, patient activity level, and lateral compartment cartilage status. Initial management for small, mildly symptomatic cysts is non-operative: relative rest, activity modification away from pivoting sports, a 2-4 week course of NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily or naproxen 500 mg twice daily with food), and structured physical therapy focusing on quadriceps and hip abductor strength, gait retraining, and proprioception. Ultrasound-guided aspiration with corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 40 mg) provides temporary symptom relief but does not address the underlying meniscal tear; recurrence rates after isolated aspiration are 50-70% within 6-12 months because the one-way valve mechanism persists. The definitive treatment for symptomatic or recurrent parameniscal cysts is arthroscopic surgery that simultaneously addresses the meniscal tear and decompresses the cyst. The two main intra-articular options are meniscal repair (preferred in younger patients with peripheral, vascularized tears) and partial meniscectomy (for non-repairable degenerate tears, removing only the unstable flap to preserve as much meniscus as possible). Cyst decompression can be performed arthroscopically from inside the joint through the tear, or via a small open lateral approach for very large or extra-capsular cysts. Combined arthroscopic decompression with repair or partial meniscectomy achieves symptomatic relief in 85-95% of patients and recurrence rates below 10% at 2-5 year follow-up. Isolated cyst excision without addressing the meniscal tear has historic recurrence rates of 30-50% and is no longer recommended. Lateral meniscus preservation matters: subtotal lateral meniscectomy doubles the risk of lateral compartment osteoarthritis at 5-10 years compared with meniscal repair. Post-operative rehabilitation typically follows a 6-12 week graduated weight-bearing and strengthening protocol, with full return to pivot sports between 3 and 6 months depending on whether repair or meniscectomy was performed.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily; naproxen 500 mg twice daily; meloxicam 15 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anti-inflammatory therapy for symptomatic but non-mechanical cysts. Taken with food for 2-4 weeks alongside activity modification. Avoid in CKD, peptic ulcer disease, anticoagulation, or uncontrolled hypertension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic pain relief in 50-65% of patients with small, low-grade cysts; minimal effect on cyst size or the underlying tear.

medicalTreatments.name: Ultrasound-guided cyst aspiration
medicalTreatments.description: Aspiration of cyst contents under sterile ultrasound guidance. Can be diagnostic, therapeutic, and bridge to surgery. Performed under local anaesthetic in clinic in 10-15 minutes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Immediate symptom relief in 70-80% of patients, but 50-70% recurrence within 6-12 months when performed alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Ultrasound-guided cyst aspiration plus intra-cyst corticosteroid (triamcinolone 40 mg or methylprednisolone 40 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Aspiration followed by corticosteroid injection into the cyst cavity. Reduces inflammatory rim and extends symptom-free interval. Limit to 2-3 injections in a single year to avoid cartilage and tendon effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Short-term symptom relief in 75-85% of patients; 12-month recurrence still 40-60% without surgical tear management.

medicalTreatments.name: Structured physical therapy (8-12 sessions over 6-10 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Quadriceps strengthening (especially vastus medialis), hip abductor and external rotator strengthening, hamstring flexibility, neuromuscular control drills, and gait retraining. Used both as primary care and post-operatively.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Functional improvement and return to activity in 50-70% of patients with mild grade I cysts; less effective once mechanical symptoms develop.

medicalTreatments.name: Activity modification and load-shifting bracing
medicalTreatments.description: Temporary avoidance of cutting sports, deep squats, and prolonged kneeling for 6-12 weeks. An off-loader lateral knee brace can be considered in selected runners with associated mild lateral compartment overload, particularly in older patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces flare frequency in 60% of recreational athletes; not a long-term solution for grade II-III cysts.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac 1% gel four times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunct for localized lateral joint-line pain when systemic NSAIDs are contraindicated. Applied directly over the cyst and joint line for 2-4 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest pain reduction in 40-55% of patients; useful in older patients with GI or renal concerns.

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic meniscal repair with intra-articular cyst decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Preferred in younger patients with peripheral, vertical, or repairable horizontal cleavage tears. The tear is repaired with all-inside or inside-out sutures and the cyst is decompressed from inside the joint through the tear, preserving meniscal tissue and load distribution.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic relief in 85-95% of patients at 2-5 years; cyst recurrence under 10%; preserves long-term cartilage protection.

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic partial lateral meniscectomy with cyst decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Used when the tear is non-repairable (complex, degenerative, or extending into the avascular inner third). Only the unstable flap is removed; the cyst is decompressed through the tear. Lateral meniscal preservation is prioritized whenever possible.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic relief in 80-90% short-term; however, partial meniscectomy of the lateral meniscus carries a 2-3 fold increased risk of lateral compartment osteoarthritis at 10 years.

surgicalOptions.name: Open cyst excision combined with arthroscopic meniscal management
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for very large (over 3 cm), extra-capsular, or recurrent grade III cysts where complete decompression cannot be achieved arthroscopically. A small lateral incision exposes and excises the cyst sac; arthroscopy concurrently addresses the meniscal tear.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic relief in 85-95%; recurrence under 10% when combined with tear management; slightly longer recovery than purely arthroscopic approach.

surgicalOptions.name: Lateral meniscus transplantation (allograft)
surgicalOptions.description: Considered in young patients (typically under 50) who have undergone subtotal lateral meniscectomy and develop persistent lateral compartment pain, instability, or early osteoarthritis. Performed in specialized sports medicine centres.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient-reported symptom improvement in 75-85% at 5-10 years; revision or conversion to arthroplasty in approximately 15-20%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection adjunct to meniscal repair
alternativeTreatments.description: Emerging evidence (level III-IV studies) suggests PRP augmentation of meniscal repair, particularly for horizontal cleavage tears, may improve healing rates by 10-15% versus repair alone. Not a standalone treatment for cysts.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture and dry needling for adjunctive pain control
alternativeTreatments.description: Small randomized trials in lateral knee pain show modest short-term pain reduction (1-2 points on VAS). Considered an adjunct to physical therapy in patients who prefer to delay or avoid injections; does not address the cyst itself.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to an orthopaedic surgeon with sports-medicine training when any of the following are present: a palpable lateral knee mass with Pisani's sign, MRI-confirmed parameniscal cyst with a horizontal cleavage tear, persistent mechanical symptoms (catching, clicking, locking), failed 6-8 weeks of conservative management, recurrence after aspiration, or features suggestive of common peroneal nerve compression. Diagnostic ambiguity (atypical mass, suspicion of vascular or neoplastic lesion) warrants urgent specialist evaluation rather than another aspiration.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a surgeon who is fellowship-trained in sports medicine or knee surgery, regularly performs both meniscal repair and decompression (ask about annual case volume — at least 30-50 meniscal surgeries per year is a reasonable benchmark), has access to high-quality MRI with musculoskeletal radiology reporting, and follows a meniscus-preservation philosophy that prioritizes repair over meniscectomy when feasible. Ask whether they perform combined arthroscopic decompression and repair in a single sitting, and what their cyst recurrence rate is at 2 years.
preventionStrategies: Maintain quadriceps and hip abductor strength with twice-weekly resistance training — strong supporting musculature reduces shear loads across the lateral meniscus during cutting and pivoting
Use sport-specific neuromuscular training programs (FIFA 11+, PEP) before competition seasons to reduce knee injury incidence by 30-50% in pivot sports
Avoid abrupt increases in training volume — apply the 10% rule (no more than 10% increase in weekly mileage or training load) to limit overuse cleavage tears
Address malalignment, leg-length discrepancy, and foot pronation with orthotics and gait assessment in symptomatic runners
Choose appropriate footwear with adequate lateral support for the sport, and replace running shoes every 500-800 km
Maintain healthy body weight — every 5 kg of excess weight adds disproportionate compressive load through the lateral compartment during single-leg stance
lifestyleModifications: Substitute high-impact pivoting sport with low-impact aerobic activity (cycling, swimming, elliptical) during symptomatic periods to maintain fitness without aggravating the cyst
Practice deep squat avoidance: when picking up objects, hinge at the hips and bend the knees only to a comfortable range (typically 90 degrees or less) until cleared post-operatively
Apply ice 15-20 minutes after activity for symptomatic days to reduce localized inflammation around the cyst
Use a knee sleeve during activity for proprioceptive feedback and mild compression — does not treat the cyst but reduces symptomatic flare-ups
Maintain hamstring and iliotibial band flexibility with daily 5-10 minute mobility work to reduce lateral load
Schedule recovery days into training weeks — chronic overuse without recovery is a leading driver of degenerative horizontal cleavage tears

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet pattern (Mediterranean-style) with olive oil, fatty fish twice weekly, leafy greens, and berries
Adequate protein (1.2-1.6 g/kg/day in active adults) to support muscle recovery and post-operative healing
Vitamin D 800-1000 IU daily if levels are low, particularly in northern latitudes — adequate vitamin D supports meniscal and cartilage health
Omega-3 fatty acids 1-2 g/day from fish or supplementation, associated with reduced systemic inflammation
Hydration of 2-3 litres of water daily to maintain synovial fluid quality
avoid: Excess refined sugar and high-fructose corn syrup, which drive systemic inflammation and weight gain
Trans fats and ultra-processed foods that promote pro-inflammatory cytokine profiles
Excess alcohol (more than 7-10 drinks per week), which impairs muscle recovery and post-surgical healing
Crash dieting around the time of surgery — adequate caloric and protein intake supports tissue repair
Smoking and tobacco — nicotine reduces meniscal and bone healing rates by 30-40% after surgery

exerciseGuidelines: During active symptoms, replace high-impact and cutting sports with cycling, swimming, elliptical, and stationary rowing 3-5 times per week. Continue closed-chain quadriceps and hip strengthening 2-3 times weekly within a pain-free range. Avoid deep squats and lunges past 90 degrees until cleared. After arthroscopic surgery, follow a graduated protocol: weight-bearing as tolerated within 1-2 weeks for partial meniscectomy or restricted weight-bearing for 4-6 weeks for repair, light aerobic work at week 4, sport-specific drills around month 3, and return to cutting sports at 4-6 months for repairs.
dailyManagement: Check the cyst weekly — note size, tenderness, and whether Pisani's sign persists
Take NSAIDs only on symptomatic days, with food, and not for more than 2-4 weeks continuously without medical review
Continue prescribed home exercise program at least 5 days per week, focusing on quadriceps and hip stability
Ice the lateral knee for 15-20 minutes after symptomatic activity
Keep a simple symptom diary noting activities that triggered flares — patterns guide activity modification
prognosis: With combined arthroscopic cyst decompression and meniscal repair or partial meniscectomy, 85-95% of patients return to their previous activity level within 4-6 months, and cyst recurrence at 2-5 years is under 10%. Isolated cyst aspiration carries a 50-70% recurrence rate at 12 months and is therefore a temporizing measure, not a definitive treatment. The long-term concern is lateral compartment osteoarthritis: the lateral meniscus carries approximately 60% of compartment load, so loss of meniscal tissue from partial meniscectomy doubles the relative risk of symptomatic lateral OA at 10 years compared with meniscal repair. Predictors of better outcome include younger age, peripheral repairable tear, intact cartilage at the time of surgery, preserved alignment, and adherence to post-operative rehabilitation. Predictors of poorer outcome include subtotal meniscectomy, advanced cartilage damage at index surgery, varus alignment with lateral compartment overload, smoking, and obesity. Most patients live with the condition silently for years before becoming symptomatic; once the cyst is symptomatic, untreated cases progress with continued mechanical symptoms and earlier osteoarthritis.
recoveryTimeline: Following arthroscopic partial meniscectomy with cyst decompression, weight-bearing as tolerated starts within days, return to driving by 1-2 weeks, return to office work by 1-2 weeks, return to running by 6-10 weeks, and return to pivot sport by 3-4 months. Following arthroscopic meniscal repair with decompression, weight-bearing is restricted or limited for 4-6 weeks, range of motion is progressed under physical therapy guidance, jogging is typically permitted at 3-4 months, and full return to pivot sport by 4-6 months. Cyst regression on follow-up MRI is usually visible within 3-6 months once the underlying tear has been addressed.
complications: Recurrence of the cyst — 30-50% if isolated cyst excision is performed without addressing the meniscal tear, under 10% if combined decompression and tear management are performed
Lateral compartment osteoarthritis — risk doubled at 10 years after subtotal lateral meniscectomy versus meniscal repair
Common peroneal nerve injury or persistent peroneal nerve irritation from large or extra-capsular cysts, presenting with lateral leg paraesthesia or foot-drop
Knee stiffness or persistent post-operative effusion in 5-10% of patients after arthroscopic repair, often responsive to extended physical therapy
Post-operative septic arthritis (0.1-0.5%) — presents with hot, swollen, painful knee and fever within 1-3 weeks of surgery and requires urgent washout
Deep vein thrombosis after knee arthroscopy in 1-2% of patients, particularly in those with additional VTE risk factors

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — Meniscus Tears
supportResources.description: Patient-facing AAOS resource covering meniscal anatomy, tear patterns, and surgical and non-surgical management.

supportResources.name: American Orthopaedic Society for Sports Medicine (AOSSM) — STOP Sports Injuries
supportResources.description: Sports-medicine education on knee injury prevention, including meniscal injuries, with athlete and parent resources.

supportResources.name: NHS — Cartilage Damage (Knee)
supportResources.description: UK National Health Service patient information on knee cartilage and meniscal injury, treatments, and recovery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Meniscus Tear (without parameniscal cyst)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A meniscus tear without a parameniscal cyst causes joint-line pain and mechanical symptoms but does not produce a palpable lateral lump. Only horizontal cleavage tears reliably generate parameniscal cysts; vertical, radial, and bucket-handle tears typically do not. MRI distinguishes the two by the presence or absence of the contiguous fluid collection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL) Tear
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ACL tears present with an acute pop, large tense haemarthrosis within hours, gross instability, and positive Lachman and pivot-shift tests. There is no palpable lateral joint-line mass, and MRI shows ligamentous discontinuity rather than meniscal fluid extravasation. The two can coexist after a pivoting injury, in which case MRI clarifies both.

confusedWithConditions.name: Knee Osteoarthritis (Lateral Compartment)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lateral compartment osteoarthritis develops gradually over years, produces activity-related pain that worsens with weight-bearing, and shows joint-space narrowing on standing X-rays. There is no discrete mass that disappears with knee flexion. Both can coexist — long-standing parameniscal cysts often herald early lateral OA.

confusedWithConditions.name: Distal Hamstring or Biceps Femoris Strain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A distal biceps femoris strain produces tenderness slightly above and behind the lateral joint line, with pain on resisted knee flexion and a history of sprinting injury. There is no palpable cystic mass and no Pisani's sign. MRI shows muscle-tendon junction oedema rather than a fluid collection adjacent to the meniscus.

relatedConditions.name: Meniscus Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: A parameniscal cyst is almost always associated with an underlying horizontal cleavage tear of the meniscus; addressing the tear is essential to prevent cyst recurrence.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Untreated lateral parameniscal cysts and partial lateral meniscectomy both predispose to lateral compartment osteoarthritis within 5-10 years.

relatedConditions.name: ACL Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: ACL injuries frequently coexist with lateral meniscus tears (terrible triad and pivot-shift mechanism); MRI workup for one should always screen for the other.

relatedConditions.name: Sports Stress Fracture
relatedConditions.relevance: Overuse pivot-sport athletes are at risk for both meniscal pathology and tibial plateau stress fractures; lateral knee pain warrants both bone and meniscal evaluation.

relatedConditions.name: Rotator Cuff Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: Like rotator cuff tears, parameniscal cysts illustrate how a fluid-filled extension of a torn fibrous structure produces a palpable mass and chronic mechanical symptoms; sports-medicine principles overlap.

faqs.question: What is a lateral meniscus cyst?
faqs.answer: A lateral meniscus cyst (parameniscal cyst) is a fluid-filled sac next to a horizontal tear of the lateral meniscus. Synovial fluid is pumped through the tear and pools outside the meniscus, forming a lump on the outer joint line. It occurs in 5-7% of lateral meniscus tears.

faqs.question: Why does the lump on the side of my knee disappear when I bend it?
faqs.answer: This is Pisani's sign. When the knee flexes, the capsule relaxes and fluid drains back through the meniscal tear, so the cyst collapses. When the knee straightens, fluid is pumped back out and the lump reappears. The sign is present in 60-80% of grade II-III cysts.

faqs.question: How common are lateral meniscus cysts?
faqs.answer: Parameniscal cysts occur in 5-7% of lateral meniscus tears versus about 1% of medial tears. The lateral side is more vulnerable because the lateral meniscus is more mobile and carries about 60% of compartment load. Most patients are 20-50 year old males in pivot sports.

faqs.question: Is a lateral meniscus cyst the same as a Baker's cyst?
faqs.answer: No. A Baker's cyst is a fluid swelling behind the knee in the popliteal fossa, usually linked to osteoarthritis. A lateral meniscus cyst sits on the outer joint line and is directly connected to a horizontal meniscal tear. MRI distinguishes them easily.

faqs.question: How is a lateral meniscus cyst diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines physical examination (palpable lateral mass with Pisani's sign) and MRI, which shows a fluid collection next to a horizontal cleavage tear. High-resolution ultrasound is an alternative when MRI is not available. X-rays help rule out other bony lesions.

faqs.question: Can a lateral meniscus cyst go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Small grade I cysts can shrink with rest and physical therapy. Cysts with a contiguous tear rarely resolve permanently because the one-way valve mechanism keeps pumping synovial fluid through the tear, so recurrence after rest or aspiration alone is common.

faqs.question: Does aspirating the cyst fix the problem?
faqs.answer: Aspiration with or without steroid gives short-term relief in 70-85% of patients but does not fix the meniscal tear. Recurrence after isolated aspiration is 50-70% within 6-12 months. It is best used for diagnostic clarity or symptom relief while planning surgery.

faqs.question: What is the success rate of surgery for a lateral meniscus cyst?
faqs.answer: Combined arthroscopic cyst decompression with meniscal repair or partial meniscectomy gives symptom relief in 85-95% of patients and cyst recurrence under 10% at 2-5 years. Isolated cyst excision without tear management has 30-50% recurrence and is not recommended.

faqs.question: Will I need a knee replacement later?
faqs.answer: Most patients treated with meniscal repair and cyst decompression do not need knee replacement. Partial lateral meniscectomy doubles the 10-year risk of lateral compartment osteoarthritis. Meniscus preservation, alignment, and weight management are the key prognostic factors.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after surgery?
faqs.answer: After partial meniscectomy with decompression, most return to office work in 1-2 weeks and running in 6-10 weeks. After meniscal repair, weight-bearing is restricted for 4-6 weeks, jogging resumes at 3-4 months, and full pivot sport at 4-6 months. PT adherence drives recovery.

faqs.question: Can I keep running with a lateral meniscus cyst?
faqs.answer: Flat moderate running may be tolerated with a grade I cyst and no mechanical symptoms. Once catching, locking, or persistent pain develops, running risks worsening the tear and accelerating cartilage damage. Cross-training with cycling or swimming is safer until the tear is treated.

faqs.question: Is a lateral meniscus cyst the same as a tumour?
faqs.answer: No. A parameniscal cyst is a benign fluid collection from a meniscal tear and is not cancer. The differential for a lateral knee mass includes soft-tissue tumours, ganglion cysts, and popliteal artery aneurysm, which is why MRI is recommended for any new, growing, or hard lateral knee lump.

faqs.question: Can children get lateral meniscus cysts?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially children with a discoid lateral meniscus, a congenital disc-shaped variant prone to horizontal cleavage tears. Discoid meniscus affects 3-5% of East Asian and around 1% of Western populations. Paediatric clicking, locking knees with a lateral lump need sports-medicine review.

faqs.question: Does a lateral meniscus cyst cause a clicking knee?
faqs.answer: Often yes. The underlying horizontal cleavage tear produces clicking and catching as the torn flap moves with flexion and extension. The cyst itself is silent, but it is a marker of the tear that clicks. Clicking with lateral joint-line pain warrants MRI rather than dismissal.

faqs.question: Will the cyst come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrence after combined arthroscopic cyst decompression with meniscal repair or partial meniscectomy is under 10% at 2-5 years. It is more likely if the underlying tear is inadequately addressed, extends into the avascular inner third, or if the patient returns to pivot sport too soon.

faqs.question: How much does treatment for a lateral meniscus cyst cost?
faqs.answer: In the US, arthroscopic meniscal surgery with cyst decompression typically costs USD 6,000-15,000 including facility, surgeon, and anaesthesia fees. In India, the same procedure runs INR 60,000-150,000 in private hospitals. NSAIDs, PT, and aspirations are far cheaper first steps.

faqs.question: Can I prevent a lateral meniscus cyst?
faqs.answer: You cannot fully prevent meniscal tears, but risk drops with strong quadriceps and hip muscles, neuromuscular programs like FIFA 11+, gradual training increases, supportive footwear, and weight management. Treating minor knee symptoms early prevents small tears from progressing to cysts.

faqs.question: What kind of doctor treats a lateral meniscus cyst?
faqs.answer: An orthopaedic surgeon with fellowship training in sports medicine or knee surgery is the right specialist. Primary care, sports physicians, and physiotherapists handle initial workup and conservative care, referring to a knee surgeon when MRI confirms a cyst with tear or PT fails.

faqs.question: Is a lateral meniscus cyst more serious than a regular meniscus tear?
faqs.answer: The cyst is benign, but it marks a horizontal cleavage tear that is harder to repair than vertical tears. Untreated, both contribute to accelerated lateral compartment osteoarthritis. Symptomatic parameniscal cysts generally warrant surgical evaluation rather than indefinite observation.

faqs.question: Can a lateral meniscus cyst cause nerve damage?
faqs.answer: Rarely. A large posterolateral cyst can compress the common peroneal nerve at the fibular head, causing lateral leg numbness, tingling, or foot-drop. This serious complication needs urgent imaging and surgical decompression to avoid permanent nerve injury.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor about a lump on the outside of my knee?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if the lump lasts more than 2-3 weeks, is painful, enlarging, accompanied by clicking or locking, or causes numbness or weakness. Any pulsatile lateral knee mass needs urgent evaluation to rule out popliteal artery aneurysm.

simpleName: Lateral Meniscus Cyst (lump on the side of the knee from a meniscus tear)
